sentences.text: All right, the only thing I still want to say is after this accident and Erica's being killed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt that I really didn't need this, on top of it, which, which is probably self-pity.
But on top of everything else, why did this have to happen to me, this-- you know, in addition to everything else.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people gave me these books written-- 'What Happens When Bad Things Happen to Good People,' which is-- and then the rabbi came to my bed in the hospital and all that.
And really, all I wanted to do is be left alone and try to, you know, get over this.

But Jill, Andy and Harris-- this is my youngest grandson-- came to Massachusetts, stayed in the house with me, and stayed with me while I was there as well as-- some of my friends took weeks off from work and came to stay with me.
And the ski club was 100% behind me.
I never-- the hospital has never-- had never seen so many cards and so many flowers in the history of the hospital in Pittsfield, Massachusetts.
So it's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's, it can make one-- I feel good knowing that people do care.
But it's something that I had to learn to live with.
And life goes on.
And the best thing that ever happened to me is my children and now my grandchildren.
I have four grandchildren.
And each one has-- they each had first a girl and then a boy.
Jill has Dina and Harris.
And Dan has-- Dina is nine, Harris is five.
He just was five.
And Dan has Lily and Jake.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dan's wife, Ursula, is Catholic.
But the children do go-- are being brought up Jewish.
They go to Hebrew school.
And they have joined a temple.
And even though Ursula did not convert, she does go to temple with the family.
My grandchildren?
Very little.
Maybe Lily might know a little, because my son is trying to push her into it.
But I feel that when they are ready, they will ask questions.
And I don't believe in upsetting the children by telling them anything now.
I mean, it's up to them.
But to have two children and have them turn out the kind of people that I've wanted them to be is enough for me.
And that's the best.
The worst was Erica.
The best is my children.
My will to live.
I never gave up, ever, at any time.
I don't know.
I don't know.
It's something being together with Dita, and we did it together.
Now, we realize now, we talk about it-- because I see her.
We meet at least once a year.
Either I go to Toronto or else she comes to here.
And she's married there.
She has three children.
She also has a bunch of grandchildren.
Her husband is a pathologist.
Neither one of us would have survived without the other.
And we both realize that.
Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we gave each other hope and support and love and friendship.
And I think that's what makes life worth, worth living.
And this is why my experiences are probably not very similar to other people's.
Because even though it was absolute hell, what I remember most is my friendship and love for Dita, of all those years.
I think you have to have it within yourself.
And whatever comes from the outside is secondary.

And you can cope with it, if you have-- if you have someone you care for, if you have certain principles that you abide by, and if you do what you feel is right and not what the world tells you to do.
I think that's more important than anything else.
No.
The world will never learn.
As a matter of fact, I was in Germany.

And when I was there in October, my cousin's daughter-- who by the way is very pro-Jewish, has been in Israel and is leaning toward Judaism, even though she was brought up Catholic-- asked me the one thing I've-- what advice would I give her more than anything else.
And I had to quote Shakespeare, and I just told her, 'To thine own self be true.' Because I think that's more important than anything.
I mean, if, if you don't do-- in order to be a person, a whole person, you have to be you.
You can't be what anybody else wants you to be.
And I try to live my life like that, and I hope that my children will.
OK.
This picture was taken in 1932.
And it's my little sister, my mother, and I.
And it was taken in Hellenthal.
It was like a family portrait.
Yes.
My mother's name was Johanna Heumann.
My sister's name was Lore.
And then my name, Margo, which is--
This picture was taken in 1935.
And it's my sister, who was Lore, who was three years old at the time.
And I was about five.
And at the time, we lived in Lippstadt.
We just had moved there.
Left, that's starting from here, right?
OK.
This picture was taken in 1940.
And it was taken in Bielefeld, the last place we lived.
And it's my sister, Lore, my father, my mother, and I'm at the other end.
This is a photo that was taken in Sweden.
I believe it was the end, probably December, '45.
And this little-- the little girl is a niece of the woman, Mrs.
Dandenell that I lived with.
And the young girl is me, the young woman.
OK, this is Marta Dandenell, the woman who took me into her home and educated me, and I lived with in Sweden.
Great person.
The picture was probably taken about 1942.
Yes, she gave me the picture.
I asked for picture.
Great way to be--
That's what I should be doing.
That's how my parents met.
Well, this picture is of Dita, taken in England in 1950.
I guess she had graduated and was a nurse at that point.
She was also a midwife.
Don't worry about that.
I'll put those back.
No problem.
Well, this is a photograph taken in 1959.
And there's Jill and Dan on my lap.
And I'm in the background.
And Dan is about two and Jill is about five.
This was taken at Dan's bar mitzvah in 1970.
And the little guy is Uncle Max, who lived to be over 100.
He is my husband's uncle.
And my mother-in-law, who is also dead now, Lillian.
And I'm in the middle.
And then there's Dan, there's Charles, my husband.
Then there's Jill.
He was-- he walked over the Brooklyn Bridge, the day it opened.
Right.
And then he told the kids about World War I, all these stories.
They used to love him.
I mean my children.
Now he was-- they were born in the United States.
They all were, yeah.
OK.
This is a photo taken in Toronto in 1978, while I was visiting with Dita.
And Dita is sitting below, and I'm above her.
She'd come in, I think.
This is a photo that was taken at our ski lodge in Waitsfield, Vermont.
And it's a work weekend, which we have every summer, to keep the lodge in tip-top shape.
This is Erica.
And it was taken about 1988.
This is the-- my retirement photo, which was taken about 1989.
It's a party that was given for me by my employer.
And it's Dan, Jill, and I.
OK.
All right.
This is my daughter, Jill.
And I have spoken about Jill on the videotape before.
And she's my oldest child.
JILL
Jill.
[LAUGHS]
Remember what?
No, you're right.
I don't remember that.
Although I believe I was there.
Yes, yes.
And the extension of it was that that meant that Hitler did not succeed.
So I have heard that.
Actually, didn't you feel the same way, more or less, when Dina was born?
Probably not to the degree that you felt it, but certainly the notion of continuity is there, yeah.
Well, I mean it's interesting, which we talked about just before you started filming this, is that I don't know a lot of her experience.
I don't know a lot of her experience.
A lot of what I've heard has been from other people, other survivors who I know or have known throughout my life.

So there's this big emotional block inside me that doesn't really know the details about it, but just, in a general sense, the experience based on some of the reading I've done and others' experiences who've shared some of it with me.
So it means a lot, obviously.
It's affected my life in different ways.
What has your mother taught you?
Rely on yourself.
[LAUGHS] A lesson my brother has learned, as well.
Well, I felt without-- that's the only chance.
That was my chance for survival, is that it's up to you, basically.
And I think it's something that I hope you teach your children, as well.

Mm hm And not so much that she's taught me, but just observing her life, that although I'm agnostic, I sometimes truly believe in the notion of angels, because she's had, in some respects, a very protected life, despite the Holocaust.
The fact that she survived and she's lived through some additional, very terrible experiences, has come out the other side.
So I really think that she may, in fact, have a guardian angel at some point, looking out for her.
Although it's exhibited in strange ways, by the fact that she survived the Holocaust but had to go through it.
I believe that.
Yeah, expensive sports, skiing and hiking.
[LAUGHS] Unfortunately, with children it's hard to do.
But the outdoors, yeah, definitely.
Sure.
I work at United Jewish Appeal, Federation of Jewish Philanthropies.
I've been there for 10 years now.
I'm in the program services department.
I direct a program that works with consultants who provide pro bono services to the agencies in New York who get funding from UJA Federation.
I also do some fund raising.
And it's very personally rewarding and meaningful to me to be there, one of the reasons I've been there for 10 years.
And I enjoy it very much.
It really gives purpose to my life in many respects.
Partially, Jill's father was the kind of person that believed that one should work for nonprofit organizations and was more or less anti-establishment.
And I am very happy that both my children are working for nonprofit organizations, to some extent my son.
Because what I actually did in advertising, even though I loved it, it was really absolutely meaningless.
And this is something I think I also tried to teach my children, that it really didn't mean anything at all and to do something worthwhile, if at all possible, with your life.
Yeah, but the added piece for me about being in the Jewish community is that I had worked in the nonprofit, nonsectarian sector.
And this has an additional meaning for me, because it's for the Jewish community.
And even though it's not something in terms of my observance, personally it's meaningful to me.
Well, I just feel that life is very short, and one should do something where someone else will benefit, which is-- there's certainly no benefit for anyone in advertising except monetary rewards.
But if you could help someone, I think that's a great thing to do.
And this is why I have been volunteering, too, now, since I have free time.
And I'm doing things to help people when I can.
Well, I work for the Big Apple Greeter, which I love.
And I should have been a tour guide, but it's too late for that now.
But I have been taking people, basically foreign people, who visit New York City for the first time.
And I show them Manhattan, because I really love Manhattan.
I'm also in a senior aide group now, where I have an old person that I visit once a week and spend like an hour, two hours with.
And this is someone who doesn't have a family and really has no contact with the outside world, except for people like me.
And I volunteer for all kinds of-- do volunteer work for a lot of sports organizations.
But basically, I feel that I have to do something to help someone else, and I think that's important.
And I think Jill is doing this in her job.
At least three times a week.
I'd say three?
I'd say five to seven.
Well, when I'm-- when I'm in the city, I swim every day.
At the Manhattan Plaza Health Club, where I have a senior membership.
But it's not very expensive.
I swim for half an hour, which is about 3/4 of a mile.
Right.
I wish I could be in the same shape.
[LAUGHS] Not.
So that's-- that's it?
That's it.
Yeah, well, I don't know if my mother told you, we tried to sort of do a oral history with a video camera, couple of years ago.
But I think Dina was too little and the camera equipment wasn't that great.
And she kept interrupting us.
And it just didn't come out, so we were going to do it again.
